evidence: The presence of deep red/brown soil ('Terra Roxa') is a definitive characteristic of the basaltic plateaus in southern/central-western Brazil (Paraná, Mato Grosso do Sul, São Paulo) and eastern Paraguay (Alto Paraná, Itapúa).
Flat agricultural landscapes dominated by large-scale farming are typical of the soy-growing regions in Paraná and Mato Grosso do Sul.
A white pickup truck used as a camera car is a highly specific clue for rural Google Street View coverage in Brazil, where Toyota Hilux and Chevrolet S10 models are frequently utilized.
Road markings consisting of double yellow center lines and white edge lines with right-hand traffic are standard for major rural roads in Brazil and Paraguay.
The vegetation mix of deciduous trees and scattered palms (such as the Jerivá or Buriti) is consistent with the transitional biomes of the Atlantic Forest and Cerrado in South America.
Decorative white fences and gates are common features at the entrances of large Brazilian 'Fazendas' (farms).
contradictions: While Misiones, Argentina, features red soil, its terrain is significantly more undulating and hilly than the flat agricultural landscape described.
The flat agricultural heartland of Argentina (the Humid Pampas) typically features dark, loamy soil (mollisols) rather than red soil.
